title: Match Bracket
subtitle: {key:%} — jump to the matching paren, brace, or bracket.
summary: % jumps from one bracket to its mate. The cursor needs to be on a bracket (or before one on the line). It's also a motion, so d% deletes a paired block.

blocks.text: Cursor on `(`? Press {key:%}. You're on the matching `)`. Press it again — back. {key:%} works for `()`, `[]`, `{}`, and (with plugins) tag pairs and language constructs.

blocks.label: Delete from here through the matching bracket

blocks.title: How % Finds the Match
blocks.text:
If the cursor is on a bracket character ({key:(}, {key:)}, {key:[}, {key:]}, {key:{}, {key:}}, plus anything in `'matchpairs'`), {key:%} jumps to its mate, respecting nesting.

If the cursor is *not* on a bracket, Vim searches forward on the current line for the *first* bracket and jumps from there. So you can be at the start of a line and press {key:%} to jump to the matching bracket of whichever pair starts the line.

**With a count**, {key:%} changes meaning entirely — see [`counts.percent-bar`](#counts.percent-bar). This is one of the few overloaded motions in Vim.

The `matchit` plugin (ships with Vim) extends {key:%} to also match `if`/`else`/`endif` in many languages, HTML/XML tags, and more. Most distros enable it by default.

{key:d}{key:%} on a `(` deletes the parens *and everything between them, inclusive* — fast way to remove a guard or a function call. Compare with {key:d}{key:i}{key:(} (contents only) and {key:d}{key:a}{key:(} (parens + contents).
